options.explanation: According to Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction, induced emf in a coil depends upon the number of turns and the rate of change of flux linking the coil. The larger the rate of change of current, the greater is emf induced in the coil.

questions.explanation: According to Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction, induced emf in a coil depends upon the number of turns and the rate of change of flux linking the coil. The larger the rate of change of current, the greater is emf induced in the coil.

questions.text: The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Varying with radial distance: This option is not correct. Inside a long solenoid, the magnetic field is essentially uniform along its central axis and does not vary with radial distance from the axis.

Uniform: The correct answer is this one. Inside a long solenoid, the magnetic field is indeed uniform. This means that the strength and direction of the magnetic field are the same at all points along the central axis of the solenoid.
Zero: This option is not correct. The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is not zero; it is nonzero and uniform.
Not enough Information: This option is not correct either. The information provided is sufficient to answer the question. Inside a long solenoid, the magnetic field is known to be uniform.

questions.explanation: The magnetic field inside a long straight solenoid-carrying current is the same at all points. It is because the magnetic field in the solenoid is constant because the lines are completely parallel to each other.As shown in figure, the magnetic field lines inside a long solenoid are straight lines, which represents uniform magnetic field.

questions.text: When a bar magnet is broken from the middle, the broken pieces will have:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: North pole only: When a bar magnet is broken in half, each piece will have both a north pole and a south pole. The magnetic field lines originate from the north pole and terminate at the south pole. Breaking the magnet doesn't remove its magnetic properties.
South pole only: Similar to option (a), when a bar magnet is broken in half, each piece will have both a north pole and a south pole. The magnetic properties are not removed by breaking the magnet.

Both south and north poles: This option is correct. When a bar magnet is broken from the middle, each broken piece will have both a south pole and a north pole. This is a fundamental property of magnets.
Magnetism will be removed: This option is not correct. Breaking a bar magnet does not remove its magnetism. Each broken piece will still retain its magnetic properties.

questions.explanation: Even if a magnet is broken in half, each half has one north pole and one south pole.

questions.text: Transformer can convert:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Low DC voltage: Transformers are primarily designed to work with alternating current (AC) rather than direct current (DC). While transformers can work with AC voltages of varying magnitudes, they are not typically used to convert low DC voltages.

High DC voltage: Similar to option (a), transformers are not commonly used to convert DC voltages, whether high or low. Transformers rely on the changing magnetic fields associated with AC currents to induce a voltage in the secondary coil.

Battery voltage: Transformers are not used to directly convert battery voltages, whether for AC or DC systems. Batteries provide a steady voltage, while transformers require alternating current to induce the changing magnetic fields necessary for their operation.

AC voltage: This option is correct. Transformers are designed to convert alternating current (AC) voltages. They can step up (increase) or step down (decrease) the voltage level while maintaining the frequency of the AC signal. Transformers work based on the principle of electromagnetic induction, where changes in the magnetic field induce a voltage in a secondary coil.

questions.explanation: Transformers can only work with AC voltage.

questions.text: Eddy currents flow in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Open loops: Eddy currents do not flow in open loops. Eddy currents are circular currents that circulate within conductive materials when subjected to changing magnetic fields. An open loop does not form a closed path for these circulating currents.

Closed loops: This option is correct. Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conductive materials. When a magnetic field changes in intensity or direction, these circulating currents are induced within the material. They can cause energy loss in transformers, motors, and other electrical devices.
Straight lines: Eddy currents do not flow in straight lines. They are circular currents that form closed loops within conductive materials.
Vertical loops: Eddy currents do not specifically flow in vertical loops. The direction of the loops depends on the orientation of the changing magnetic field and the conductive material.

questions.explanation: Eddy currents are loops of electrical current induced within conductors by a changing magnetic field in the conductor according to Faraday's law of induction. Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conductors, in planes perpendicular to the magnetic field.

questions.text: When the coil is rotated in the magnetic field, the current produced is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Constant: If a coil is rotated in a magnetic field at a constant speed, a sinusoidal alternating current (AC) is induced in the coil. The magnitude of the current changes sinusoidally over time, but the frequency remains constant.

Decrease: As the coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the induced current can decrease if the speed of rotation decreases or if the magnetic field weakens. This decrease in the rate of change of magnetic flux will result in a decrease in the induced current.

Increase: As the coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the induced current can increase if the speed of rotation increases or if the magnetic field strength increases. This leads to a higher rate of change of magnetic flux, which induces a larger current.

Continuously changing: This option is correct. When a coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the induced current is continuously changing due to the constantly changing magnetic flux. The direction and magnitude of the induced current depend on the speed and direction of rotation.

questions.explanation: Direction of current changes as the coil is rotated.

questions.text: In an AC generator, AC reverses its direction _ times per second.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: 30: This option suggests that the AC current reverses its direction 30 times per second. However, this is not accurate.
40: This option suggests that the AC current reverses its direction 40 times per second. However, this is not accurate.

50: This option suggests that the AC current reverses its direction 50 times per second. This is the correct answer. AC current in many regions and power systems around the world is typically supplied at a frequency of 50 Hz, which means it changes direction (reverses) 50 times per second.

60: This option suggests that the AC current reverses its direction 60 times per second. While 60 Hz is also a common frequency for AC power in some regions, the most widely used standard frequency is 50 Hz.

questions.explanation: The frequency of an AC generator is 50 times per second.Hence, the current reverses its direction 50 times.

questions.text: An AC generator converts:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. An AC generator does not convert mechanical energy into heat energy directly. While some heat may be generated due to friction in the generator's moving parts, the primary purpose of the generator is to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy through the process of electromagnetic induction, as explained in option B.

An AC (alternating current) generator, also known as an alternator, converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. This process involves rotating a coil of wire (the rotor) within a magnetic field, which induces an alternating current in the wire due to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. The mechanical energy required to rotate the rotor is typically provided by a turbine, engine, or other mechanical sources.Therefore, option B) Mechanical energy to electrical energy is the correct choice.

This option is incorrect. An AC generator does not convert electrical energy into mechanical energy. Instead, it operates in the opposite direction, converting mechanical energy (from sources such as turbines, engines, etc.) into electrical energy, as explained in option B.

This option is also incorrect. An AC generator does not convert heat energy directly into electrical energy. While some heat may be generated during the operation of the generator due to friction and losses in the system, the primary conversion process involves mechanical energy being converted into electrical energy through electromagnetic induction, as explained in option B.

questions.explanation: A generator can convert mechanical/kinetic energy to electrical energy. It can produce either AC or DC current. An AC generator creates an alternating current that periodically reverses direction. But in a DC generator, a direct current flows in one direction.

questions.text: Fluorescence is the property of absorbing radiant energy of …... frequency and re-emitting energy of _ frequency in the visible region of electromagnetic spectrum.
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: In fluorescence, a photon is absorbed which takes an atom or molecule to a higher energy level. To conserve momentum, this higher level of energy might consist of a combination of electronic transition, rotational transition, vibrational transition, and kinetic energy. The atom or molecule may remain in a higher energy state for a long enough period of time to collide with other atoms or molecules and transfer some of the energy to them. When a photon of lower energy is emitted, the remaining energy may also be emitted as a photon, but it is unlikely that the energy and momentum can both be conserved, so much of the time the energy remains in the molecule until a collision happens to exchange the energy by means of a collision. Eventually, a low-energy photon is emitted, but by then some of the energy has become part of the internal energy of the material, raising its temperature ever so slightly. So, some energy is released as a second photon and some of the energy contributes to heating up the fluorescent material.

questions.text: In an experiment, a conductor of length 1,0 m is placed perpendicular to an external magnetic field. The given graph shows how the force on the conductor varies as the current through the conductor changes. The magnitude of the magnetic field applied in this experiment will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Given the graph showing a straight line from (0,0) to (3,0.7) with a constant gradient, we need to determine the magnitude of the magnetic field applied in the experiment. From the graph, we can see that the force (F) on the conductor is directly proportional to the current (I) because the graph shows a straight line. This means that as the current increases, the force also increases proportionally. Since the length of the conductor (L) is given as 1.0 m, we can calculate the magnetic field (B) by finding the slope of the line from the graph. The slope represents the change in the dependent variable (force) divided by the change in the independent variable (current). In this case, the slope represents the ratio of force to current. From the graph, we can calculate the slope as the change in force (0.7 N) divided by the change in current (3 A): Slope = (Change in Force) / (Change in Current) = (0.7 N) / (3 A) ≈ 0.23 N/A Therefore, option A is the correct answer based on the given options.

questions.text: What is not true regarding magnetic force and magnetic intensity?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: If an electron's movement is parallel to the magnetic field, it will experience no force.
options.explanation: When a charged particle moves parallel to the magnetic field, the magnetic force acting on it is zero because the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field is 0 degrees, making sin(θ) = sin(0) = 0.

options.text: If an electron's movement is parallel to the magnetic field, it will rotate anticlockwise.
options.explanation: The electron will not rotate at all if it moves parallel to the magnetic field, as the force is zero in such a scenario.

options.text: If an electron enters perpendicular to the magnetic field, it will move in a circular path.
options.explanation: When an electron enters perpendicular to the magnetic field, it experiences a force that is perpendicular to both its velocity and the magnetic field, causing it to move in a circular path.

options.text: If an electron enters perpendicular to the magnetic field, the force experienced will be maximum.
options.explanation: When an electron enters perpendicular to the magnetic field, the magnetic force is at its maximum. This is because the angle is 90 degrees, hence sin(θ) = sin(90) = 1, maximizing the force as per the equation F = qvB sin(θ).

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: If an electron enters perpendicular to the magnetic field, the force experienced will be maximum. This is because the magnetic force on a charged particle is given by the equation F = qvB sin(θ), where θ is the angle between the velocity vector and the magnetic field vector. The force is maximal when θ = 90 degrees, meaning the electron enters perpendicular to the field.Option A is incorrect because no force is exerted when the movement is parallel to the magnetic field. Option B is incorrect as there is no force to cause rotation in this orientation. Option C is incorrect because entering perpendicular doesn't make the path parallel to the plane; instead, it results in circular motion.

questions.text: If a stationary bar magnet is placed near a coil so that maximum lines of force passes through the coil, the galvanometer shows:

The galvanometer will show no current when a stationary bar magnet is placed near a coil so that the maximum lines of magnetic force pass through the coil.When the magnet is stationary and its magnetic field is not changing, there will be no induced electromotive force (EMF) and therefore no induced current in the coil. The galvanometer will show no current.For induced current to flow, there must be a changing magnetic field. If you were to move the magnet relative to the coil, such as by sliding it back and forth, or rotating it, the magnetic field passing through the coil would change, and this changing magnetic field would induce an EMF and a corresponding induced current in the coil. The magnitude of the induced current would depend on factors such as the rate of change of the magnetic field and the properties of the coil.

Magnetic flux is the measure of the number of magnetic field lines passing through a surface, not directly related to the force experienced by a moving charge.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Magnetic Field. A magnetic field is defined by the force it exerts on moving charges. The force is proportional to the velocity of the charge and the strength of the magnetic field.Option A (Gravitational Field) is incorrect because it deals with forces due to gravity, not magnetic forces.Option C (Magnetic Flux) is incorrect because it describes the number of magnetic field lines passing through a surface, not the force on a moving charge.Option D (Flux) is too general and does not specifically relate to magnetic fields affecting moving charges.

questions.text: An electron enters in a magnetic field at an angle less than 90 degrees with magnetic field. Which of the following path is followed by an electron?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An electron would follow a straight path if its velocity is completely parallel to the magnetic field lines (θ=0 or θ=180). In this case, there is no perpendicular component of velocity to cause circular or helical motion.

An electron will follow a circular path when it enters the magnetic field at an angle of 90 degrees to the field lines. This is because the entire velocity is perpendicular to the field, creating a centripetal force that results in circular motion.

A curved path is not a complete description. While the electron initially follows a curved trajectory when entering the field, it ultimately forms a helical path due to the combination of circular and linear motion.

When an electron enters a magnetic field at an angle between 0 and 90 degrees, its motion is helical. This is due to the presence of both perpendicular and parallel components of velocity relative to the magnetic field. The perpendicular component leads to circular motion, while the parallel component allows for linear motion, resulting in a helical path.

questions.explanation: When an electron enters a magnetic field at an angle less than 90 degrees, its velocity can be decomposed into components: one parallel and one perpendicular to the magnetic field. The perpendicular component of the velocity causes the electron to move in a circular path due to the Lorentz force, while the parallel component causes it to move linearly along the direction of the magnetic field. The combination of these two motions results in a helical path.The option 'Straight' is incorrect because it applies only when the velocity is completely parallel to the magnetic field. 'Circular' is incorrect as this occurs only when the velocity is entirely perpendicular to the field. 'Curve' is partially correct initially but does not describe the continuous motion, which is helical.

questions.text: If a certain region's magnetic field is directed vertically up and a proton enters this field from the left side, it will go _.

The proton enters from the left, so its initial velocity is directed right. However, the magnetic force is perpendicular to this direction.

If a certain region's magnetic field is directed vertically up, and a proton enters this field from the left side, it will experience a force perpendicular to both its velocity and the magnetic field direction, according to the right-hand rule. Applying the right-hand rule, if you extend your right-hand fingers in the direction of the magnetic field (vertically up) and your thumb in the direction of the proton's velocity (from left to right), your palm will point in the direction of the force experienced by the proton. In this case, the force will be directed downward. Therefore, the proton will be deflected downward as it enters the region with the vertically upward-directed magnetic field. Using the Fleming’s left hand rule, the direction of the force on the proton is towards us (out of the page).

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. When a proton enters a magnetic field vertically upward from the left side, it experiences a force perpendicular to both the field and its velocity. By applying the right-hand rule, where your fingers point in the direction of the magnetic field (up) and your thumb in the direction of the proton's velocity (right), your palm faces towards you, indicating the direction of the force. Thus, the proton will be deflected towards us, out of the paper.Options A, B, and C are incorrect because they do not account for the perpendicular nature of the magnetic force as predicted by the right-hand rule. The force is not in the direction of the magnetic field (up), nor opposite (down), nor in the initial velocity direction (right).

questions.text: The SI unit of magnetic flux is Weber which is equivalent to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The unit NA/m is the unit of magnetic field strength, also known as magnetic field intensity or magnetic field intensity. It represents the SI unit for magnetic field strength and is measured in amperes per meter (A/m).

The unit NmA is not a recognized unit in the SI system. Newton-meter (Nm) is the SI unit of torque or moment of force, representing the product of a force (measured in newtons) and the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation (measured in meters). Milliampere (mA) is the SI unit for electrical current, representing one thousandth of an ampere. Combining the two units, NmA, would not have a specific meaning or representation in a recognized SI unit.

The unit Nm^-1A^-1 represents the reciprocal of the unit for electrical resistance, which is ohm (Ω). Reciprocal of ohm: (Ω)^-1 = Ω^-1 = Nm^-1A^-1 Therefore, Nm^-1A^-1 is the unit of electrical conductance, which is the measure of how easily electric current can flow through a material. It is commonly denoted by the unit siemens (S) in the SI system.

The SI unit of magnetic flux is indeed the Weber (Wb). The Weber is defined as the amount of magnetic flux that passes through a surface of one square meter (1 m²) perpendicular to a magnetic field of one Tesla (1 T). In terms of base SI units, one Weber is equivalent to one Tesla meter squared (T·m²).

questions.text: A particle having mass m and charge q moves in a circular path in a magnetic field of strength B. If the radius of the circular path is r, the time required by this particle to complete one revolution is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The time required for a particle to complete one revolution in a circular path of radius r can be calculated using the formula: T = 2πr/v where v is the velocity of the particle. In a magnetic field of strength B, the force on a charged particle q with velocity v is given by F = qvB. The force provides the necessary centripetal force mv²/r, so we have: qvB = mv²/r Solving for v, we get: v = (qBr/m) Substituting this value of v in the formula for T, we get: T = 2πr/v = 2πm/qB Therefore, the time required by the particle to complete one revolution is 2πm/qB. Answer A is correct.
This formula is incorrect because it erroneously includes the radius r in the denominator, which is not present in the correct formula for the period.
This expression represents the angular frequency, not the period. The period should be expressed as the reciprocal of the angular frequency, which is not the case here.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: 2πm/qB. When a charged particle moves in a magnetic field, the magnetic force acts as the centripetal force required for circular motion. The force on the particle is given by qvB, which equals the centripetal force mv²/r. By equating and solving for the velocity v, we find v = qBr/m. Substituting this into the formula for the period, T = 2πr/v, results in T = 2πm/qB.Option B includes an unnecessary factor of r in the denominator, Option C represents angular frequency instead of the period, Option D incorrectly includes an extra factor of q in the numerator, and Option E omits the necessary factor of π.

questions.text: Power transfer from primary to secondary is through flux linkage, so the primary and secondary coils should be wound in such a way that flux coupling between them is _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the flux coupling between the primary and secondary coils were minimized, it would result in poor energy transfer between the coils. In such a scenario, only a small portion of the magnetic flux generated by the primary coil would link with the secondary coil. As a result, the efficiency of the transformer would be significantly reduced, leading to lower power transfer and potential performance issues.

Maintaining a constant flux coupling between the primary and secondary coils would imply that the amount of magnetic flux linking the coils remains the same regardless of changes in operating conditions or load. While it's important to maintain a stable flux linkage for consistent transformer performance, a constant flux coupling may not be practical or achievable in all cases. In practice, the flux coupling can vary depending on factors such as load changes, transformer design, and magnetic properties of the core material.

Zero flux coupling between the primary and secondary coils would mean that there is no magnetic interaction between them. In such a situation, there would be no energy transfer from the primary to the secondary coil, rendering the transformer non-functional. Without flux linkage, there would be no induced voltage in the secondary coil, and thus, no power would be transferred between the coils. Therefore, zero flux coupling is not desirable for transformer operation.

This can be achieved by ensuring that the coils are placed close to each other and that they have a high number of turns. Additionally, the coils should be wound in opposite directions to ensure that the magnetic fields generated by the currents flowing through them add up, resulting in a stronger overall field. By optimizing the winding configuration, engineers can achieve efficient power transfer and minimize losses due to leakage flux.

questions.explanation: This can be achieved by ensuring that the coils are placed close to each other and that they have a high number of turns. Additionally, the coils should be wound in opposite directions to ensure that the magnetic fields generated by the currents flowing through them add up, resulting in a stronger overall field. By optimizing the winding configuration, engineers can achieve efficient power transfer and minimize losses due to leakage flux.Therefore, option D is the correct choice.

Cast steel is also unsuitable due to higher magnetic losses compared to silicon steel.

questions.text: The attraction capacity of electromagnet will increase if the
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: a) Core length increases:In an electromagnet, the core is typically made of ferromagnetic material that enhances the magnetic field when current passes through the coil. When the core length increases, it provides more material for the magnetic field lines to pass through, which can increase the attraction capacity of the electromagnet. So, this option is correct.

b) Core area increases:Increasing the core area does not directly impact the attraction capacity of the electromagnet. The magnetic field strength is mainly determined by the number of turns in the coil and the amount of current flowing through it, rather than the core area.

c) Flux density decreases:Flux density refers to the amount of magnetic field lines passing through a given area. If the flux density decreases, it means there are fewer magnetic field lines, resulting in a weaker magnetic field and reduced attraction capacity. So, this option is incorrect.

d) Flux density increases:When the flux density increases, it means more magnetic field lines pass through a given area, resulting in a stronger magnetic field. A stronger magnetic field will increase the attraction capacity of the electromagnet. So, this option is correct.

questions.explanation: Flux density represents the number of magnetic field lines passing through a cross section. More field lines passing means a greater magnetic field will be produced. A stronger magnetic capacity therefore means that more magnetic field lines are passing through a surface.

questions.text: Two long straight parallel wires held vertically have equal but opposite currents as shown in the figure. Which of the following effects will be observed?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Magnetic field at ‘X’ is stronger than that at ‘Y’ and ‘Z’
Magnetic field at ‘X’ is weaker than that at ‘Y’ and ‘Z’
Magnetic field at ‘X’, ‘Y’ and ‘Z’ is same
Magnetic field at ‘X’ is weaker than that at ‘Y’ but stronger than that at ‘Z’.
Magnetic field at ‘Y’ is stronger than that at ‘X’ and ‘Z’
options.explanation: When two parallel wires carry equal and opposite currents, they create a magnetic field around them. The direction of the magnetic field at any point is given by the right-hand rule. Now, let's consider the magnetic field at points X, Y, and Z. At point Y, the magnetic fields created by the two wires are in the same direction and add up, resulting in a stronger magnetic field than at either X or Z.At point X, the magnetic field due to the current in the left wire is in the upward direction, and the magnetic field due to the current in the right wire is in the downward direction. These two magnetic fields oppose each other, resulting in a weaker magnetic field than at point Y.At point Z, the magnetic field due to the current in the right wire is in the upward direction, and the magnetic field due to the current in the left wire is in the downward direction. These two magnetic fields also oppose each other, resulting in a weaker magnetic field than at point Y.Therefore, the magnetic field at Y is stronger than that at X and Z.

These two magnetic fields also oppose each other, resulting in a weaker magnetic field than at point Y.Therefore, the magnetic field at Y is stronger than that at X and Z.

questions.text: Who introduced the laws of electromagnetic induction?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Genghis Khan was a Mongolian emperor and military leader who lived during the 12th and 13th centuries. He was known for his conquests and the establishment of the Mongol Empire, which was the largest contiguous empire in history. However, he is not associated with the laws of electromagnetic induction. Genghis Khan's achievements lie primarily in the realm of military conquest and empire-building, rather than scientific discoveries or contributions to physics.

Heinrich Friedrich Emil Lenz was a German physicist who lived in the 19th century. Lenz is known for formulating Lenz's law, which states that the direction of an induced current in a conductor will be such that it opposes the change that produced it. While Lenz's law is related to electromagnetic induction, he did not introduce the fundamental laws of electromagnetic induction. These laws were discovered and formulated by Michael Faraday.

Isaac Newton was an English mathematician, physicist, and astronomer who lived in the 17th and 18th centuries. Newton made significant contributions to the fields of physics, mathematics, and optics, including his laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation. However, he is not credited with introducing the laws of electromagnetic induction. The laws of electromagnetic induction were formulated by Michael Faraday in the early 19th century.

Michael Faraday, a British physicist and chemist, introduced the laws of electromagnetic induction. He discovered these laws through experiments conducted in the early 19th century. Faraday's work laid the foundation for our understanding of electromagnetism and the principles of generating electricity from magnetism. The laws of electromagnetic induction, formulated by Faraday, describe how a changing magnetic field induces an electromotive force (EMF) and a current in a nearby conductor. These laws are fundamental to the operation of electrical generators and transformers, as well as many other electromechanical devices. Therefore, option D) Michael Faraday is the correct choice.

questions.explanation: Michael Faraday introduced the law of electromagnetic induction. Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction states that a current is induced in a conductor which is in a changing magnetic field. In accordance with Faraday's first law, any minute variation in the magnetic field of the coil will result in an emf which is getting induced in the coil.

questions.text: Transformer can convert:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Low DC voltage: Transformers are primarily designed to work with alternating current (AC) rather than direct current (DC). While transformers can work with AC voltages of varying magnitudes, they are not typically used to convert low DC voltages.

High DC voltage: Similar to option (a), transformers are not commonly used to convert DC voltages, whether high or low. Transformers rely on the changing magnetic fields associated with AC currents to induce a voltage in the secondary coil.

Battery voltage: Transformers are not used to directly convert battery voltages, whether for AC or DC systems. Batteries provide a steady voltage, while transformers require alternating current to induce the changing magnetic fields necessary for their operation.

AC voltage: This option is correct. Transformers are designed to convert alternating current (AC) voltages. They can step up (increase) or step down (decrease) the voltage level while maintaining the frequency of the AC signal. Transformers work based on the principle of electromagnetic induction, where changes in the magnetic field induce a voltage in a secondary coil.

questions.explanation: Transformers can only work with AC voltage.

questions.text: Eddy currents flow in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Open loops: Eddy currents do not flow in open loops. Eddy currents are circular currents that circulate within conductive materials when subjected to changing magnetic fields. An open loop does not form a closed path for these circulating currents.

Closed loops: This option is correct. Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conductive materials. When a magnetic field changes in intensity or direction, these circulating currents are induced within the material. They can cause energy loss in transformers, motors, and other electrical devices.
Straight lines: Eddy currents do not flow in straight lines. They are circular currents that form closed loops within conductive materials.
Vertical loops: Eddy currents do not specifically flow in vertical loops. The direction of the loops depends on the orientation of the changing magnetic field and the conductive material.

questions.explanation: Eddy currents are loops of electrical current induced within conductors by a changing magnetic field in the conductor according to Faraday's law of induction. Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conductors, in planes perpendicular to the magnetic field.

questions.text: When the coil is rotated in the magnetic field, the current produced is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Constant: If a coil is rotated in a magnetic field at a constant speed, a sinusoidal alternating current (AC) is induced in the coil. The magnitude of the current changes sinusoidally over time, but the frequency remains constant.

Decrease: As the coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the induced current can decrease if the speed of rotation decreases or if the magnetic field weakens. This decrease in the rate of change of magnetic flux will result in a decrease in the induced current.

Increase: As the coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the induced current can increase if the speed of rotation increases or if the magnetic field strength increases. This leads to a higher rate of change of magnetic flux, which induces a larger current.

Continuously changing: This option is correct. When a coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the induced current is continuously changing due to the constantly changing magnetic flux. The direction and magnitude of the induced current depend on the speed and direction of rotation.

questions.explanation: Direction of current changes as the coil is rotated.

questions.text: An AC generator converts:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. An AC generator does not convert mechanical energy into heat energy directly. While some heat may be generated due to friction in the generator's moving parts, the primary purpose of the generator is to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy through the process of electromagnetic induction, as explained in option B.

An AC (alternating current) generator, also known as an alternator, converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. This process involves rotating a coil of wire (the rotor) within a magnetic field, which induces an alternating current in the wire due to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. The mechanical energy required to rotate the rotor is typically provided by a turbine, engine, or other mechanical sources.Therefore, option B) Mechanical energy to electrical energy is the correct choice.

This option is incorrect. An AC generator does not convert electrical energy into mechanical energy. Instead, it operates in the opposite direction, converting mechanical energy (from sources such as turbines, engines, etc.) into electrical energy, as explained in option B.

This option is also incorrect. An AC generator does not convert heat energy directly into electrical energy. While some heat may be generated during the operation of the generator due to friction and losses in the system, the primary conversion process involves mechanical energy being converted into electrical energy through electromagnetic induction, as explained in option B.

questions.explanation: A generator can convert mechanical/kinetic energy to electrical energy. It can produce either AC or DC current. An AC generator creates an alternating current that periodically reverses direction. But in a DC generator, a direct current flows in one direction.

questions.text: A conductor of length L is moved across a magnetic field. Due to this activity, a potential difference appears across the ends of the conductor. This type of potential difference is termed as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Self-Induction: Self inductance is the induction of a voltage in a current-carrying wire when the current in the wire itself is changing. In the case of self-inductance, the magnetic field created by a changing current in the circuit itself induces a voltage in the same circuit. Therefore, the voltage is self-induced

Mutual Induction :When two coils are brought in proximity with each other the magnetic field in one of the coils tend to link with the other. This further leads to the generation of voltage in the second coil. This property of a coil which affects or changes the current and voltage in a secondary coil is called mutual inductance.

Motional EMF : Motional emf is the emf induced by the movement of the conductor through the magnetic field.ORAn emf induced by motion relative to a magnetic field is called a motional emf. This is represented by the equation emf = LvB, where L is length of the object moving at speed v relative to the strength of the magnetic field B.

Electrostatic potential : The electric potential (also called the electric field potential, potential drop, the electrostatic potential) is defined as the amount of work energy needed to move a unit of electric charge from a reference point to the specific point in an electric field.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Motional EMF. When a conductor of length L moves across a magnetic field B, a potential difference appears across its ends, which is termed as motional EMF. This is induced by the movement of the conductor through the magnetic field. Self Induction and Mutual Induction involve induction of voltage in coils due to changing currents and proximity to other coils, respectively. Electrostatic potential relates to the work energy needed to move electric charge in an electric field.

questions.text: If a constant current is flowing through a coil, then the flux through that coil will become constant. In such types of arrangements, the electromotive force (emf) induced in the coil will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Self-SInductance :The self-induced emf is also called the back emf as it opposes any change in the current in a circuit. Physically, the self-inductance plays the role of inertia. It is the electromagnetic analogue of mass in mechanics
Zero :In the case given above there is a constant flow of current which means there is no change in flux so the induced E.M.F is zero because induced emf depends upon the time rate change of flux.

Mutual-Inductance :When two coils are brought in proximity with each other the magnetic field in one of the coils tend to link with the other. This further leads to the generation of voltage in the second coil. This property of a coil which affects or changes the current and voltage in a secondary coil is called mutual inductance.

Back EMF : When the armature of a d.c. motor rotates under the influence of the driving torque, the armature conductors move through the magnetic field and hence e.m.f. is induced in them as in a generator. The induced e.m.f. acts in opposite direction to the applied voltage V (Lenz's law) and in known as back or counter e.m.f.

questions.explanation: The emf induced in a circuit is directly proportional to the time rate of change of the magnetic flux through the circuit. And if there is no change in flux so the induced emf is zero.

questions.text: A solenoid is cut into two halves. Magnetic induction due to the same current in each half will be:

options.explanation: The magnetic induction due to a current-carrying solenoid depends on factors such as the number of turns per unit length, the current flowing through it, and the permeability of the material within the solenoid. questions.text: The function of a main transformer is to convert:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: one direct voltage to another direct voltage of different magnitude.
options.explanation: This type of voltage conversion is not possible using a transformer because transformers work only with alternating voltages, not direct voltages.

options.text: one alternating voltage to another alternating voltage of different magnitude.
options.explanation: This is the primary function of a transformer. The transformer can increase or decrease the magnitude of the alternating voltage without changing the frequency. The ratio of the number of turns in the primary coil to the number of turns in the secondary coil determines the voltage ratio.

options.text: a high value alternating voltage to low value direct voltage.
options.explanation: This type of voltage conversion is not possible using a transformer because transformers work only with alternating voltages, not direct voltages.

options.text: A high value alternating current to low value direct voltage

questions.text: The unit of magnetic flux is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Tesla (T) is the SI unit of magnetic field strength (magnetic induction). One Weber of magnetic flux corresponds to one Tesla of magnetic field.

The unit of magnetic flux is the Weber (Wb). The Weber is the International System of Units (SI) unit for magnetic flux and is defined as the magnetic flux that, when linking a circuit of one turn, produces an electromotive force of one volt in the circuit if it is reduced to zero at a uniform rate in one second.
Gauss (G) is a unit of magnetic field often used in the centimeter-gram-second (CGS) system. 1 Gauss is equal to 10^(-4) Tesla.
Henry (H) is the SI unit of inductance, which is a measure of how an electric current induces a magnetic field in a circuit. It is not a unit of magnetic flux.

questions.explanation: The unit of magnetic flux is the Weber (Wb).

options.explanation: Lenz's law is a principle in electromagnetism that states that an induced current will always flow in such a direction as to oppose the change that produced it. While Lenz's law is related to the concept of electromagnetic induction, it is not directly associated with the principle behind transformers.

Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction states that a change in the magnetic field through a loop of wire induces an electromotive force (emf) in the wire. This law is closely related to the principle behind transformers. However, it is not the specific principle that transformers are based on.

Magnetic induction, also known as electromagnetic induction, is the fundamental principle behind the operation of transformers. According to this principle, when the current in the primary coil of a transformer changes, it creates a changing magnetic field. This changing magnetic field induces a voltage in the secondary coil of the transformer, which results in the transfer of electrical energy between the primary and secondary coils.

Ampere's law relates the magnetic field around a closed loop to the electric current passing through the loop. While Ampere's law is an important principle in electromagnetism, it is not directly associated with the operation of transformers.

questions.explanation: Magnetic induction, also known as electromagnetic induction, is the fundamental principle behind the operation of transformers. According to this principle, when the current in the primary coil of a transformer changes, it creates a changing magnetic field. This changing magnetic field induces a voltage in the secondary coil of the transformer, which results in the transfer of electrical energy between the primary and secondary coils.

questions.text: If 0.5 T field is applied over an area of 2-meter square which lies at an angle of 60 degree with the field, then the resulting flux will be:

options.explanation: This option suggests that the resulting flux will be equal to the magnetic field strength (0.5 T). However, the flux depends on both the magnetic field strength and the area but is affected by the angle between them.

This option suggests that the resulting flux will be 0.5 Weber (Wb). Weber is the unit of magnetic flux, and this option directly provides the correct unit. However, we need to calculate the actual value.
This option suggests that the resulting flux will be 0.25 Weber (Wb). It is half the value provided in option B.
This option suggests that the resulting flux will be 0.25 Tesla (T), which is the magnetic field strength. It does not take into account the effect of the angle between the field and the area.

questions.explanation: The correct statement is B. 0.5 Wb. To calculate the magnetic flux, you need to use the formula: Magnetic Flux (Φ) = Magnetic Field Strength (B) × Area (A) × cos(θ) Given: Magnetic Field Strength (B) = 0.5 T Area (A) = 2 m² Angle (θ) = 60 degrees Φ = 0.5 T × 2 m² × cos(60°) Φ = 0.5 T × 2 m² × 0.5 Φ = 0.5 Wb So, the resulting magnetic flux is indeed 0.5 Weber (Wb).

questions.text: The induced emf produced in the coil is sometimes called as?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Self-inductance refers to the property of a coil or circuit to induce an electromotive force (emf) in itself when the current flowing through it changes. It is often denoted by the symbol L and is related to the coil's ability to oppose changes in current.

Back emf (electromotive force) is a term commonly used to describe the induced emf in a coil or motor when the motor is in motion. It opposes the applied voltage and is responsible for counteracting the flow of current in certain electrical devices.
Motional emf is induced emf that occurs in a conductor when it moves through a magnetic field. This emf is produced due to the relative motion of the conductor and the magnetic field.

Mutual inductance refers to the property of two coils or circuits to induce emf in each other when there is a change in current in one of the coils. It is a measure of the ability of one coil to influence the other.

questions.explanation: The correct statement is B. Back emf. Back emf is often used to describe the induced emf in a coil or motor, especially in the context of motors and generators.

questions.text: The magnitude of the magnetic flux through area A has exactly the same value at angles:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is correct because at these angles, the magnetic flux through area A is equal in magnitude. At 0°, the magnetic field is fully aligned with the area, while at 180°, it is aligned but in the opposite direction, resulting in the same magnitude of flux.

This option is incorrect because both 270° and 360° yield zero magnetic flux. Although they are equal in value, they do not match the positive flux values found at 0° or 180°.

questions.explanation: The magnitude of magnetic flux (Φ) through a surface is given by the formula Φ = B·A·cos(θ), where B is the magnetic field strength, A is the area, and θ is the angle between the magnetic field and the normal to the surface. For angles of 0° and 180°, cos(0°) = 1 and cos(180°) = -1, indicating that the magnitude of the flux remains the same, although the direction is opposite.In contrast, at 90°, the flux becomes zero because the angle leads to cos(90°) = 0. Similarly, at 270°, the flux is also zero due to the same cosine factor. Thus, options B, C, and D are incorrect as they do not yield the same non-zero flux values as found at 0° and 180°.

questions.text: A 3-cm length of wire is moved at right angles across a uniform magnetic field with a speed of 2.0 m/s. If the flux density is 5.0 teslas, what is the magnitude of the induced e.m.f?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The other options A ,C and D are incorrect because they do not account for the correct application of Faraday's Law and the given values.

To calculate the induced electromotive force (EMF) in this scenario, we can use Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, which states that the induced EMF is equal to the negative rate of change of magnetic flux. Formula:EMF = - (dΦ/dt)Where: EMF is the induced electromotive force (in volts) dΦ/dt is the rate of change of magnetic flux (in webers per second)Breaking down the problem:Magnetic flux (Φ): The magnetic flux is given by the product of the magnetic field strength (B) and the area (A) perpendicular to the field. In this case, the wire is moving perpendicular to the field, so the area is simply the length of the wire times its width (which we'll assume is negligible compared to its length). Φ = B * A = B * L * wRate of change of magnetic flux: Since the wire is moving at a constant speed, the area it sweeps through per second is constant. Therefore, the rate of change of magnetic flux is simply the product of the magnetic field strength and the speed of the wire. dΦ/dt = B * L * vSubstituting values:B = 5.0 TL = 3 cm = 0.03 mv = 2.0 m/sCalculation: EMF = - (dΦ/dt) = - (B * L * v) = - (5.0 T * 0.03 m * 2.0 m/s) = -0.3 VThe magnitude of the induced EMF is 0.3 volts.Therefore, the correct answer is Option 2: 0.30 V.
The other options A ,C and D are incorrect because they do not account for the correct application of Faraday's Law and the given values.
The other options A ,C and D are incorrect because they do not account for the correct application of Faraday's Law and the given values.

questions.explanation: To calculate the induced electromotive force (EMF) in this scenario, we can use Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, which states that the induced EMF is equal to the negative rate of change of magnetic flux. Formula:EMF = - (dΦ/dt)Where: EMF is the induced electromotive force (in volts) dΦ/dt is the rate of change of magnetic flux (in webers per second)Breaking down the problem:Magnetic flux (Φ): The magnetic flux is given by the product of the magnetic field strength (B) and the area (A) perpendicular to the field. In this case, the wire is moving perpendicular to the field, so the area is simply the length of the wire times its width (which we'll assume is negligible compared to its length). Φ = B * A = B * L * wRate of change of magnetic flux: Since the wire is moving at a constant speed, the area it sweeps through per second is constant. Therefore, the rate of change of magnetic flux is simply the product of the magnetic field strength and the speed of the wire. dΦ/dt = B * L * vSubstituting values:B = 5.0 TL = 3 cm = 0.03 mv = 2.0 m/sCalculation: EMF = - (dΦ/dt) = - (B * L * v) = - (5.0 T * 0.03 m * 2.0 m/s) = -0.3 VThe magnitude of the induced EMF is 0.3 volts.Therefore, the correct answer is Option 2: 0.30 V.

questions.text: When a transformer is connected to 120-volt ac, it supplies 300 V to a device. The current through the secondary winding then is 0.06 A and the current through the primary is 2 A. The number of turns in the primary winding is 400. The number of turns in the secondary winding is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. If the number of turns in the secondary winding were 1200, the voltage ratio would be 3:1, not 2.5:1.

To calculate the number of turns in the secondary winding, we can use the relationship between the voltage, current, and number of turns in a transformer:Formula:(Vs / Vp) = (Ns / Np)Where: Vs is the voltage in the secondary winding Vp is the voltage in the primary winding Ns is the number of turns in the secondary winding Np is the number of turns in the primary winding We are given: Vs = 300 V Vp = 120 V Np = 400 turns Is = 0.06 A Ip = 2 Substituting the values into the formula: (300 V / 120 V) = (Ns / 400 turns) Simplifying: 2.5 = (Ns / 400 turns) Solving for Ns: Ns = 2.5 * 400 turns = 1000 turnsTherefore, the number of turns in the secondary winding is 1000 turns.This is the correct answer, as calculated above.
This option is incorrect. If the number of turns in the secondary winding were 800, the voltage ratio would be 2:1, not 2.5:1.

options.text: Deflection to the left and right with decreasing amplitude
options.explanation: As the magnet oscillates in and out of the coil, it induces an electromotive force (EMF) in the coil due to the changing magnetic flux. This induced EMF causes a current to flow in the coil, which is detected by the galvanometer.The galvanometer will show:- Deflection to the left when the magnet is moving into the coil (increasing magnetic flux)- Deflection to the right when the magnet is moving out of the coil (decreasing magnetic flux)The amplitude of the deflection will decrease over time due to the damping of the oscillations (energy loss due to friction, air resistance, etc.).

questions.text: Magnetic flux is maximum when the angle between the magnetic field and the normal to the plane of finite area is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Magnetic flux (Φ) through a finite area is given by:Φ = B × A × cos(θ)where:B = magnetic field strengthA = area of the planeθ = angle between the magnetic field and the normal to the planeWhen θ = 0°, the magnetic field is parallel to the normal, and cos(0°) = 1. This means the magnetic flux is maximum, since the field lines pass through the area at a 90° angle, resulting in the maximum amount of flux.
When θ = 90°, the magnetic field is perpendicular to the normal, and cos(90°) = 0. This means the magnetic flux is zero.
When θ = 60°, the magnetic flux is reduced, since cos(60°) = 0.5.
When θ = 30°, the magnetic flux is also reduced, since cos(30°) ≈ 0.87.

questions.text: The ability to produce an electromotive force by changing the magnetic field inside a coil is used to generate.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: While mechanical energy can be used to generate electricity (e.g., through a turbine), the ability to produce an EMF by changing the magnetic field is specifically used to generate electricity, not mechanical energy.

The ability to produce an electromotive force (EMF) by changing the magnetic field inside a coil is the principle behind electromagnetic induction, which is used to generate electricity. This is the basis for many electric generators, including power plants, dynamos, and transformers.When the magnetic field inside a coil changes, an electric current is induced in the coil. This is known as electromagnetic induction. The changing magnetic field can be achieved by:- Moving a magnet near the coil- Rotating the coil within a magnetic field- Changing the current in a nearby coil
Electromagnetic force is a fundamental force of nature, but it's not what's being generated in this context.
Magnetic energy is not being generated; rather, the changing magnetic field is used to generate electricity.

questions.text: The current produced by a generator is called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A generator produces AC, not DC
While AC can be described as pulsating, this term is not a specific type of current.

A generator, also known as an alternator, produces an alternating current (AC). AC is a type of electric current that periodically reverses direction, oscillating at a specific frequency. This is in contrast to direct current (DC), which flows in one direction only.
This term is too general and doesn't accurately describe the type of current produced by a generator.
